idea.hook: How LONG would it take to reach the Milky Way's supermassive black hole?
facts: Sagittarius A*, the black hole at our galaxy's center, is 26,000 light-years from Earth.
Our fastest spacecraft, the Parker Solar Probe, travels at 195 km/s (430,000 mph).
At that speed, it would take nearly 40 MILLION years to reach the Galactic Center.
idea.payoff: Even at light speed, the journey would still take 26,000 years – revealing the unimaginable scale of our own cosmic home!
idea.title: Milky Way's Core: An ETERNITY Away? 🤯🌌

scenes.text: How LONG to reach the Milky Way's Black Hole?
scenes.visual: Distant, awe-inspiring view of a spiral galaxy (Milky Way representation) with a bright core.

scenes.text: Sagittarius A* is 26,000 LIGHT-YEARS from Earth.
scenes.visual: Simplified diagram of the Milky Way, showing Earth's position relative to the Galactic Center, with a highlighted distance arc.

scenes.text: Our fastest probe (Parker Solar Probe) travels at 195 km/s.
scenes.visual: Illustration of the Parker Solar Probe against a starfield, with a subtle speed trail effect.

scenes.text: At that speed, it would take nearly 40 MILLION YEARS!
scenes.visual: A numerical countdown/spinner that quickly resolves to '40,000,000' with 'YEARS' below it, against a backdrop of slowly moving stars.

scenes.text: Even at light speed: 26,000 years. UNIMAGINABLE SCALE.
scenes.visual: Stunning, high-contrast visual of a black hole's accretion disk, slowly rotating, then zooming out to reveal a vast, star-filled cosmic vista.
